narrative: As a white male applicant with a middle-income family background, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicting their likelihood of passing on the first try. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.50 (below the typical 3.21 average), LSAT score of 29 (well under the 36.70 average), full-time enrollment plans, and school decile rankings from your early years—and estimated a 60% probability that you would fail the bar exam. This high risk of failure is why your application was rejected; law schools use this prediction to prioritize admits likely to succeed and pass the bar promptly. The three biggest factors driving this decision were your Year 1 grade decile of 1 (putting you in the bottom 10% of your school, far below the average decile of about 6), your Year 3 grade decile of 2 (still in the lower ranks compared to the typical 5.5 average), and your choice of full-time study. Your low Year 1 decile had the strongest pull toward predicting failure, as it signals early academic struggles that often persist through law school and the bar. Similarly, the Year 3 decile reinforced this concern, showing grades that stayed in the lower end of your school's distribution. On a more positive note, planning full-time enrollment nudged the prediction slightly toward success, aligning with the vast majority of students (over 90%) who pursue that path and tend to perform better. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your early and mid-year deciles are notably weaker, while your LSAT and GPA also trail averages, compounding the model's worries despite your full-time commitment. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a middle-income background—many in similar circumstances succeed with targeted improvements—but the model saw patterns in your academics mirroring those who struggle on the bar. To move forward, focus on retaking the LSAT to boost it toward 35 or higher, strengthening your academic record through additional coursework or a post-bac program to improve future decile rankings, and gathering strong letters of recommendation that highlight your resilience. Reapplying after these steps could shift your predicted odds dramatically, opening doors at schools that value growth stories like yours. Keep pushing; many students turn early setbacks into bar-passing triumphs.
